reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: Excellent 'When should I use this?' section with a concrete alternatives table — exactly what a startup CTO scanning options needs to make a fast decision.

Pricing is grounded, specific, and verified against source throughout (Fargate cost table, API Gateway vs ALB crossover point at ~500k requests/day, NAT Gateway cost). Numbers match the distilled pricing source precisely.
Load balancing comparison table is the standout section — surfaces the most impactful architectural decision clearly with feature/pricing/protocol tradeoffs in a scannable format.

Code examples are realistic and copyable, with focus markers ([!code focus-start/end]) drawing attention to the relevant configuration. The basic example includes both Stacktape config and application code.
Good use of Warning and Tip components to surface critical caveats (CORS only with API Gateway, firewall requires ALB, NAT cost). These prevent common mistakes for the target audience.
Source-grounded defaults throughout: scaling policy 80%, log retention 90 days, health check intervals, minInstances/maxInstances both default to 1. All verified against TypeScript source types.
problems: <PropertiesTable definitionName="WebServiceConfig" /> uses a definitionName that does not exist in the config schema. The schema contains 'WebService', 'WebServiceProps', 'WebServiceAlbLoadBalancingProps', etc. — but not 'WebServiceConfig'. The component will render null, leaving the API reference section completely empty.

The gradual deployments section lists strategy names (Canary10Percent5Minutes, Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes, etc.) but provides no code example showing how to configure them. For a CTO who wants to ship safely, this is the section most likely to prompt a 'how do I actually do this?' follow-up.

The 'environment' property is a common configuration need (API keys, feature flags, secrets) but is never shown in any code example. The only environment variable mechanism shown is connectTo, which only covers inter-resource connections.

Headings use noun-phrase form ('Load balancing', 'Scaling', 'Custom domains') rather than question form ('How do I configure load balancing?') which reduces SEO/AEO discoverability per the style guide's recommendation for question-format H2/H3 headings where natural.
mandatoryFixes: Replace <PropertiesTable definitionName="WebServiceConfig" /> with <PropertiesTable definitionName="WebServiceProps" /> (or the equivalent valid definition name from the schema). 'WebServiceConfig' does not exist in the config schema definitions and the component returns null.

Add a code example to the gradual deployments section showing the 'deployment' property with at least one strategy (e.g., Canary10Percent5Minutes) inside a <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> block.
optionalImprovements: Add a code example showing the 'environment' property with manual environment variables (e.g., API keys, feature flags) alongside or near the connectTo example. This is one of the first things a developer configures.
Convert key headings to question form where natural for better SEO: 'How do I configure load balancing?', 'How does scaling work?', 'How do I attach a custom domain?'.
Briefly mention or link to EFS volume mounts (volumeMounts property) — persistent storage is a real concern for stateful web services and is currently only implicitly referenced via side containers.

Add a brief note about the stopTimeout property (default 2 seconds, range 2-120) near the health checks or side containers section — a 2-second default is surprisingly short and can cause issues with graceful shutdown of database connections or in-flight requests.
Consider showing an ARM/Graviton example in the resources section since the Tip mentions 20% savings but doesn't show how to configure it (architecture: 'arm64').

reviewerResults.persona: SMB engineering manager with medium to high AWS knowledge. Cares about long-term maintainability, team clarity, operational fit, technically correct Stacktape class-based config examples, and valid MDX component usage.
strengths: Excellent load balancing comparison table with pricing, feature matrix, and clear per-option recommendations — exactly what an engineering manager needs for an architecture decision.

Strong pricing context throughout: Fargate cost table by vCPU/memory tier, NAT gateway cost warnings, CDN per-request pricing, and load balancer cost crossover point (~500k requests/day). All sourced from the provided pricing data.
Well-structured 'When should I use a web service?' section with a concrete alternatives table — helps the reader decide before they invest time reading the rest of the page.
Code examples use [!code focus-start/end] markers effectively, drawing the reader's eye to the relevant configuration without hiding surrounding context.
Production-relevant advice woven into the right places: minInstances: 2 recommendation in scaling, NAT Gateway cost warning behind a <Warning>, ARM cost savings in a <Tip>.
Static outbound IP example correctly includes the required `stackConfig.vpc.nat` alongside `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT` — avoids a common deploy-time surprise.
problems: <PropertiesTable definitionName="WebServiceConfig" /> — `WebServiceConfig` does not appear in the provided source types. The source defines `WebService` and `WebServiceProps` (which extends `SimpleServiceContainer`). This may render an empty or incorrect API reference.

Gradual deployments section describes strategy names (Canary10Percent5Minutes, Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes, AllAtOnce) and validation hooks in prose but provides no config example showing the `deployment` property. An engineering manager evaluating this feature cannot copy-paste anything.

Side containers section is too thin: it describes the two container types but shows no configuration example. The source types (`ServiceHelperContainer`) have `containerType`, `packaging`, and other properties the reader would need to configure.

No mention of the `environment` property for setting custom environment variables directly on the web service. The page covers `connectTo` well but omits the primary mechanism for injecting API keys, feature flags, and other non-resource config.

EC2 compute is mentioned as an alternative to Fargate in the Resources section but has no config example. An engineering manager comparing cost models would want to see how to specify `instanceTypes`.

Missing coverage of the `alarms` and `disabledGlobalAlarms` properties. The source types show these are first-class config options on WebServiceProps, supporting both HTTP API Gateway and ALB alarm triggers.
mandatoryFixes: Verify and correct <PropertiesTable definitionName="WebServiceConfig" /> — this definitionName does not match any TypeScript type in the provided source files (WebService, WebServiceProps, SimpleServiceContainer are the relevant interfaces). Use the correct definitionName from the source types or the generated schema.

Add a config example to the gradual deployments section showing the `deployment` property with at least one strategy (e.g., Canary10Percent5Minutes). The current prose-only treatment is not actionable.
Add a config example to the side containers section showing at least a `run-on-init` container (the most common use case — database migrations).
optionalImprovements: Add a short subsection or inline example for the `environment` property, showing how to set custom environment variables alongside `connectTo`.
Add an EC2 compute example in the Resources section showing `instanceTypes` configuration, with a brief note on when EC2 is more cost-effective than Fargate for steady workloads.

Mention `stopTimeout` (default 2 seconds, range 2-120) in the health checks section or near side containers — relevant for services that need graceful shutdown (draining connections, flushing buffers).
Add a brief mention of `alarms` configuration with a pointer to the alarms/monitoring page, since alarm setup is a common concern for engineering managers running production services.
In the health checks section, add a small config example for `internalHealthCheck` to make the two health check types equally actionable.
The Fargate pricing table could add a row for the 8 vCPU / 16384 MB tier to cover GPU-adjacent workloads that some teams run on Fargate.
Consider adding a 'Logging and observability' sentence that mentions `stacktape logs --follow` for tailing — the current logging section mentions the command but not the follow flag.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer with little AWS or DevOps experience. Needs jargon kept under control and wants examples that are easy to follow.
strengths: Opening paragraph is a strong, self-contained 40-word answer — works well for AI extraction and sets clear expectations immediately.
The 'When should I use a web service?' section with the alternatives table is excellent for decision-making. A beginner can quickly determine if this is the right resource.

Load balancing comparison table is the highlight of the page — surfaces pricing, features, and protocol support in a single scannable view, which is exactly the information needed for the biggest architectural decision.
Pricing context is well-grounded in the source data (~$9/month for minimal Fargate, ~$1/million for API Gateway, ~$18/month for ALB) and consistently referenced throughout relevant sections.
Code examples use [!code focus-start/end] annotations effectively to highlight the specific property being discussed while keeping full context visible.

Static outbound IP section correctly includes the required `stackConfig.vpc.nat` configuration alongside `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT`, preventing a common pitfall where users enable the flag but forget the VPC config.
Warnings are placed precisely where they prevent mistakes — CORS only with http-api-gateway, firewall only with ALB, NAT Gateway cost.
problems: PropertiesTable uses `definitionName="WebServiceConfig"` but the provided source types define `WebService`, `WebServiceProps`, and `StpWebService` — no `WebServiceConfig` type exists in the source files.

Route53, ACM, and ARN are used without brief explanations. For a developer with little AWS experience, 'Your domain must have a Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account' is opaque. A one-liner like 'Route53 is AWS's DNS service' would help.

The gradual deployments section lists specific strategy names (Canary10Percent5Minutes, Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes, AllAtOnce) but the provided source only shows `deployment?: ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` without the enum values — these claims are unverifiable from the given source.

The `environment` property is a key configuration area (listed in `SimpleServiceContainer`) that most users will need, but it has no dedicated section or example. Users will need to discover it only through the API reference.

The health checks section states internal health check defaults (interval 30s, timeout 5s, 3 retries) but the provided source for `ContainerHealthCheck` type is not included — these defaults are unverifiable.
The `<FeatureComparisonTable>` component is used but not documented in the style guide or instructions as a known MDX component — verify it exists in the docs framework before publishing.
mandatoryFixes: Use the correct definitionName from the source types for `<PropertiesTable>`. The source defines `WebServiceProps` (the properties interface) and `WebService` (the top-level interface) — not `WebServiceConfig`. Update to match whichever name the schema generator produces, and verify it renders correctly.

Add a brief inline explanation when first using Route53 ('Route53, AWS's DNS service') and ACM ('ACM, AWS's certificate manager') — the target audience has little AWS experience and these terms gate understanding of the custom domains section.
optionalImprovements: Add a short 'Environment variables' subsection or mention near the basic example showing how to pass custom env vars via the `environment` property. This is one of the first things users configure and is currently only discoverable through the API reference.
In the gradual deployments section, add a minimal CodeBlock example showing the `deployment` property configuration — it is the only major feature section without a code example.

Consider adding a one-line note in the basic example about the `PORT` environment variable: Stacktape injects it automatically, and the app must listen on it. The Hono example uses `process.env.PORT` but doesn't explain why.
The side containers section could benefit from a short example showing a run-on-init migration container — this is the most common use case and currently has no code.

In the scaling section, mention what metric triggers scale-in vs scale-out more concretely. The current 'scale out when average CPU or memory exceeds 80%' is good but linking to how `keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder: 70` overrides this default would be clearer.
The Fargate CPU/memory table is helpful but could note that invalid combinations (e.g., 0.25 vCPU with 4096 MB) will fail deployment — beginners may try arbitrary values.

Consider briefly mentioning `stopTimeout` (default 2s) near the health checks or scaling section — a 2-second graceful shutdown is surprisingly short and catches many users off guard with in-flight request failures.

issues.statement: The `cors` property configures Cross-Origin Resource Sharing headers at the load balancer level, overriding any CORS headers set by your application code.
issues.reason: CORS is configured at the HTTP API Gateway level, not at a generic "load balancer level." The draft's own warning later clarifies that managed CORS only works with http-api-gateway. Calling it "load balancer level" is misleading since http-api-gateway is not a load balancer — it's an API gateway. This phrasing could confuse readers who use an ALB into thinking CORS is managed there.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "The `cors` property configures Cross-Origin Resource Sharing headers at the API Gateway level, overriding any CORS headers set by your application code."

evidence.quote:
#### CORS settings. Overrides any CORS headers from your application.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Only works with `http-api-gateway` load balancing (the default).

issues.statement: Use it for REST APIs, GraphQL APIs, web applications, WebSocket servers, and any workload that needs to handle HTTP traffic continuously.
issues.reason: WebSocket support requires loadBalancing type application-load-balancer. The opening paragraph lists WebSocket servers as a general use case without mentioning that the default load balancing (http-api-gateway) does not support WebSockets. A reader following the basic example would not get WebSocket support.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a parenthetical: "WebSocket servers (requires Application Load Balancer)" or move WebSocket mention to a later section where ALB is discussed.

evidence.quote:
- **`application-load-balancer`**: Flat ~$18/month + usage. Required for gradual deployments
   *   (`deployment`), firewalls (`useFirewall`), and WebSocket support.

issues.statement: The CDN has no monthly base cost — you pay per request (~$0.01/10k) and per GB transferred.
issues.reason: The CDN pricing (~$0.01/10k requests) is not present in the provided source files. The aws-price distilled source for web-service only covers Fargate pricing. The "no monthly base cost" claim for CloudFront and the specific per-request pricing are not grounded in any provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the specific CDN pricing numbers, or qualify with "see AWS CloudFront pricing" since the pricing data is not in the provided source files. Alternatively, just say "CDN pricing is usage-based with no monthly base cost."

evidence.quote:
**AWS Fargate** (used by `web-service`, `private-service`, `worker-service`, `multi-container-workload`, `batch-job`):
- vCPU billing: ~$7.29/month per 0.25 up to ~$466/month per 16.
- Memory billing: ~$1.60/month per 512 MB up to ~$384/month per 122880 MB.

issues.statement: When `cors` is enabled with no other options, it uses permissive defaults (`*` origins, common headers).
issues.reason: The HttpApiCorsConfig type is not provided in the source files. The claim about permissive defaults with `*` origins cannot be verified from the provided sources. The behavior may be correct but is not grounded.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify the CORS defaults against the HttpApiCorsConfig type or API Gateway CORS source and either confirm or remove the specific claim about `*` origins.

issues.statement: Gradual deployments: Canary — send 10% of traffic to the new version first, then shift all traffic after a validation window... Linear — increase traffic to the new version by 10% at regular intervals... AllAtOnce — instant switch with automatic rollback on failure
issues.reason: The draft describes the strategies in general terms but omits the specific strategy value names that users must actually write in config. Users need to know the enum values (Canary10Percent5Minutes, Canary10Percent15Minutes, Linear10PercentEvery1Minutes, Linear10PercentEvery3Minutes, AllAtOnce). The draft lists these in parentheses but doesn't clarify they are the actual config values.
issues.suggestedFix: Make it clearer that the parenthetical values are the actual config values, e.g., by showing a brief code example or a small table mapping strategy type to config value.

issues.statement: You can optionally run validation Lambda functions before and after the traffic shift to perform smoke tests.
issues.reason: While this claim is accurate (beforeAllowTrafficFunction and afterTrafficShiftFunction exist on ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig), the property names and mechanism are not shown. Users reading this section don't learn how to actually configure this — a brief mention of the property names or a code snippet would be more actionable.
issues.suggestedFix: Mention the property names: "Set `beforeAllowTrafficFunction` and/or `afterTrafficShiftFunction` to the name of a Lambda function in your stack."

positiveFindings: Fargate pricing calculations are accurate: 0.25 vCPU (~$7.29) + 512 MB (~$1.60) ≈ $9/month matches the source data, and all other rows in the pricing table are consistent.
Load balancing types (http-api-gateway, application-load-balancer, network-load-balancer) and their capabilities are accurately described and match the source type definitions.
ALB health check defaults (path /, interval 5s, timeout 4s) are correctly stated per @default JSDoc annotations in WebServiceAlbLoadBalancingProps.
NLB port defaults (protocol TLS, containerPort defaults to port) match WebServiceNlbLoadBalancingPort source.
Internal health check defaults (interval 30s, timeout 5s, 3 retries) verified against ContainerHealthCheck type in multi-container-workloads.d.ts.
Scaling defaults (minInstances: 1, maxInstances: 1, 80% CPU/memory threshold) verified against ContainerWorkloadScaling type.
Logging retention default of 90 days and retentionDays property name verified against ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging type.
Architecture property ('x86_64' default, 'arm64' option, ~20% cheaper) verified against source types and pricing data.
EC2 'refreshed weekly for patching' claim verified — implemented via AWS Scheduler cron(0 0 ? * SUN *) calling startInstanceRefresh.
PropertiesTable definitionName='WebServiceConfig' is valid — confirmed as the docs-system type name configured in docs/scripts/generate-docs/pages.ts.
All five gradual deployment strategy names match the ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig enum exactly.
Side container types (run-on-init, always-running) and their behavior descriptions match ServiceHelperContainer source.
connectTo format (STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]) and example variables match ScriptEnvProps documentation.
NAT Gateway pricing (~$32/month) and availabilityZones (1-3) match NatSettings source.
Custom domain properties (domainName, customCertificateArn, disableDnsRecordCreation) all verified against DomainConfiguration type.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> — no forbidden YAML or fenced TypeScript patterns.
Application code (Hono server example) correctly uses a fenced code block without intellisense.
stopTimeout default of 2 seconds and enableRemoteSessions default of false match source @default annotations.
ReferenceableParams resource='web-service' matches the WebService type value.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded overall with accurate pricing calculations, correct defaults, and proper use of MDX components. Most technical claims — scaling defaults, health check defaults, logging retention, deployment strategies, architecture options, and even the EC2 weekly patching claim — are verified against source types and implementation code. Three medium-severity issues found: (1) CORS described as configured at the "load balancer level" when it's actually at the API Gateway level, (2) WebSocket listed as a general use case in the opening paragraph without mentioning the ALB requirement, and (3) CDN pricing numbers (~$0.01/10k requests) not present in provided source files. Three low-severity issues around insufficient detail in gradual deployment strategy documentation and unverifiable CORS defaults.

suggestions: Rewrite generic noun-phrase headings as question or action formats to match real search queries. 'Scaling' → 'How do I auto-scale a web service?', 'CDN' → 'How do I add a CDN to my web service?', 'CORS' → 'How do I configure CORS?', 'Logging' → 'How are logs collected and retained?', 'Firewall' → 'How do I protect my web service with a firewall?', 'Custom domains' → 'How do I attach a custom domain?'. This aligns headings with the queries users actually type.

Rename 'Basic example' to something search-discoverable like 'How do I deploy a web service?' or 'Minimal deployment example'. 'Basic example' is invisible to search — nobody searches for that phrase.

Add a short FAQ section (3-5 items) at the bottom with structured data potential. Good candidates: 'How much does a Stacktape web service cost?', 'Can a web service scale to zero?', 'How do I add WebSocket support to a web service?', 'What is the difference between API Gateway and Application Load Balancer?', 'How do I get a static outbound IP for my container?'. These are questions the page already answers but aren't surfaced in a scannable FAQ format.

The opening paragraph is strong but doesn't mention 'Stacktape' as the subject until the second sentence. Lead with 'A Stacktape web service is an always-on container...' so the paragraph works as a standalone snippet even when extracted without the page title for context. AI engines often strip titles.

The 'Resources and compute' heading competes poorly with search intent. Split into 'How do I choose CPU and memory?' (covering Fargate sizing and the pricing table) and 'Fargate vs EC2: which compute engine should I use?' — these match two distinct queries users actually ask.

In the Gradual deployments section, add a brief code example showing the deployment property configuration. Currently it's the only feature section without a code block, which makes it less useful as a standalone extracted answer and less likely to appear in code-search results.
